abstract: This review summarizes five decades of on-field concussion biomechanics research in American football, from early helmet instrumentation in the 1960s through NFL laboratory reconstructions, college and high school Head Impact Telemetry (HIT) system studies, and youth football investigations. Key findings include that head impact exposure and concussion tolerance both increase with player age, and that substantial differences exist between helmet models in their ability to reduce concussion risk.
tldr: On-field biomechanical research across all levels of American football has produced validated helmet rating systems that show significant differences between helmet models in concussion risk reduction, providing actionable data for equipment selection and policy decisions.
methods: Narrative review of on-field biomechanical data collection studies across NFL (laboratory reconstructions and video analysis), collegiate and high school (HIT system, CARE Consortium), and youth football levels. Covers sensor technologies, injury metrics (HIC, SI, HIP, STAR, DAMAGE, UBrIC), risk function development, and helmet evaluation methodologies.
findings:
• Average head kinematics for concussive impacts: NFL ~98±28g linear, 6432±1813 rad/s² rotational; college/high school ~104±30g, 4726±1931 rad/s²
• Head impact exposure and injury tolerance both increase with player age
• Youth players show lower concussion tolerance than adults
• Linemen sustain highest frequency of impacts; skill positions sustain higher magnitude impacts
• Helmet performance varies substantially between models; STAR rating system quantifies these differences
• Practice structure modifications (reducing tackling drill time) can reduce high-magnitude impacts by 38%
• Mouthguard sensors more tightly coupled to skull, offering more accurate head kinematics than helmet-mounted sensors
limitations: Concussions are vastly underreported in football; helmet-mounted sensors measure helmet, not head, acceleration; NFL reconstruction data overestimated risk due to sampling bias toward injurious impacts; youth data limited by small concussion sample sizes.
practicalImplications: Athletic trainers and sport science staff should use validated helmet rating data (STAR methodology) to guide helmet selection at all levels. Practice structure modifications that reduce high-magnitude head impacts are evidence-based interventions. Individual-level head impact monitoring should account for player position and personal exposure history.
athleteDev: Biomechanical data supports position-specific head impact profiling: linemen require highest-priority helmet performance, while skill position players face fewer but higher-magnitude impacts. Practice design should minimize unnecessary high-magnitude head impacts through drill modification and contact limitations during preseason.
rtp: Biomechanical threshold data informs understanding of impact severity associated with concussion diagnosis and can guide graduated RTP protocols. The finding that concussed players sustain greater head impact exposure on days of injury supports monitoring of cumulative exposure as part of pre-injury baseline and RTP decision frameworks.

citation: Tucker S, Milne I, Pitcher M, et al. A review of syndesmosis injuries and preferred treatment in football players. Curr Rev Musculoskelet Med. 2025. DOI: 10.1007/s12178-025-09954-x.
abstract: This narrative review examines syndesmotic ankle injuries in American football players at the professional and collegiate levels, covering anatomy, injury mechanisms, epidemiology, diagnosis, and treatment options. Syndesmotic injuries account for 24.6% of all ankle sprains in NCAA football players and carry a 9–16 week average recovery period in the NFL. The review concludes with a surgical algorithm and RTP protocol, finding flexible fixation (suture button) generally preferred over rigid fixation.
tldr: Syndesmotic ankle injuries are common and highly disabling in American football players, with MRI as the diagnostic gold standard and flexible fixation generally preferred over rigid fixation, though rigid fixation may be warranted for high-risk positions such as linemen.
methods: Narrative review of epidemiology, anatomy, biomechanics, diagnosis, and treatment of syndesmotic injuries in football athletes. West Point Ankle Grading System used as classification framework. Three institutional case examples presented with surgical technique details.
findings:
• Syndesmotic injuries account for 24.6% of NCAA ankle sprains and 16% of NFL Combine ankle injury history
• NFL recovery averages 9–16 weeks; syndesmosis with fracture median RTP 250 days in NFL
• Artificial turf significantly increases syndesmotic injury incidence (2.9 vs. 2.2 per 10,000 AE on grass)
• Linemen and running backs at greatest risk; tackling (31%) and blocking (22%) most common mechanisms
• Flexible fixation shows similar clinical outcomes to rigid fixation with less implant failure and hardware removal
• MRI is gold standard; overall RTP rate after adequately treated syndesmotic injury exceeds 90%
• Direct arthroscopic visualization of reduction is superior to fluoroscopy alone
limitations: Review article without systematic meta-analysis; case examples represent a single institution; indications for flexible versus rigid fixation remain subjective; long-term outcomes data in high-demand football athletes are limited.
practicalImplications: Athletic trainers should recognize that syndesmotic injuries carry significantly longer RTP timelines than lateral ankle sprains (9–16 weeks vs. days–weeks). Physical exam findings combined with MRI are necessary for accurate diagnosis. Postoperative rehab protocol spans 14–16 weeks before full RTP.
athleteDev: NFL and collegiate programs should implement position-specific risk awareness for syndesmotic injuries, with linemen and running backs at highest risk. Turf surface management and technique coaching may reduce incidence. Pre-participation screening at the NFL Combine should include thorough ankle syndesmosis assessment.
rtp: Syndesmotic injuries require staged RTP: 2 weeks splint, 2–6 weeks boot, weeks 6–12 functional rehab and strengthening, weeks 14–16 jogging and conditioning with full RTP as strength and proprioception normalize. Syndesmosis with fracture or diastasis represents the longest RTP timeline (median 250 days in NFL).

citation: Oliver JM, Jones MT, Anzalone AJ, et al. A season of American football is not associated with changes in plasma tau. J Neurotrauma. 2017;34(23):3295-3300.
abstract: This longitudinal study examined plasma tau concentrations in 19 NCAA Division I football athletes over the 2014–2015 season across eight serial blood samples, comparing them to 19 non-contact swim athlete controls. Despite routine sub-concussive head impacts throughout the season, no significant changes in plasma tau were observed, and no group differences were detected between football athletes and swim athletes.
tldr: Despite routine sub-concussive head impact exposure over a full American football season, plasma tau concentrations did not change significantly in collegiate football athletes, suggesting plasma tau is not sensitive enough to detect axonal damage from repetitive sub-concussive impacts at this level of play.
methods: Longitudinal observational cohort study; 19 NCAA Division I football athletes underwent serial fasting plasma tau sampling at 8 time points over 27 weeks (pre-season through post-season). 19 NCAA swim athletes provided a single comparison sample. Athletes classified as starters vs. non-starters. Plasma tau measured via Simoa HD-1 analyzer.
findings:
• No significant change in plasma tau across the football season regardless of starter status
• No significant difference between football athletes and swim controls after non-contact or contact periods
• Football starters had higher tau than non-starters at baseline (T1; p=0.039) and late season but not compared to swim athletes
• Tau AUC was higher for starters vs. non-starters (p=0.052), approaching but not reaching significance
• No concussions occurred in the football cohort during the study
limitations: Small sample size (n=19 football, n=19 swim); absence of concurrent biomechanical head impact data; no concussion diagnoses during study; single plasma sample from swimmers precludes longitudinal comparison; blood sampling window may miss acute tau peaks.
practicalImplications: Plasma tau does not appear to be a reliable real-time biomarker for monitoring sub-concussive impact burden in collegiate American football athletes. Other markers such as serum neurofilament light (NF-L) may offer greater sensitivity for sub-concussive axonal injury detection.
athleteDev: Routine plasma tau monitoring over a season is unlikely to yield actionable data for athlete health management related to sub-concussive exposure. Biomarker-based monitoring programs should consider NF-L or other markers with demonstrated sensitivity.
rtp: Plasma tau does not serve as a useful RTP biomarker for concussion or sub-concussive impact assessment. Clinicians should not rely on tau normalization as an RTP criterion. Future biomarker-assisted RTP protocols should incorporate markers with demonstrated acute sensitivity such as NF-L, GFAP, or S100B.

citation: Kluczynski MA, Kelly WH, Lashomb WM, Bisson LJ. A systematic review of the orthopaedic literature involving National Football League players. Orthop J Sports Med. 2019;7(8):2325967119864356.
abstract: This systematic review summarized all data published between January 1980 and March 2018 on orthopaedic injuries experienced by NFL Combine candidates and professional NFL players. A total of 147 articles met inclusion criteria and were divided into 11 anatomic categories. The majority of studies (74%) were level 4 evidence, with knee (24%), spine (13%), shoulder (13%), and foot (12%) injuries most commonly examined. Data sources included the NFL Combine database (26%), internet searches (24%), and the NFL Injury Surveillance System (22%).
tldr: This is the first comprehensive systematic review of orthopaedic injuries in NFL players, covering 147 studies across 11 anatomic sites; knee, spine, and shoulder injuries dominate the literature, most evidence is level 4, and internet-based data sources likely undercount true injury rates compared to official surveillance systems.
methods: Systematic review of PubMed, Embase, and CINAHL databases (January 1980–March 2018); included orthopaedic injury studies in NFL Combine participants and professional NFL players; excluded concussions, TBI, facial injuries, vascular injuries, and case reports; articles categorized by anatomic site, level of evidence, and data source.
findings:
• 147 articles included across 11 anatomic categories
• Most studied sites: knee (24%), spine (13%), shoulder (13%), foot (12%)
• 74% of studies were level 4 evidence; no level 1 or 2 studies identified
• ACL RTP ranged 60–90% depending on position; performance diminished post-ACL in most positions except QB
• Shoulder instability decreased NFL Combine draft likelihood and career longevity
• Cervical disc herniation negatively affected games played; RTP ~70% after ACDF
• Achilles tendon rupture RTP 66–72%; performance metrics decreased 2–3 years post-repair
• Internet-based studies identified far fewer injury cases than NFLISS-based studies
limitations: Majority of studies are level 4 evidence; internet-based data sources are incomplete; 17% of studies collected data from a single team; survey data subject to recall bias; no level 1 or 2 studies exist for any orthopaedic injury category in NFL players.
practicalImplications: Team physicians and athletic trainers should reference this review as a single-source summary of injury frequency and RTP outcomes by anatomic site. Medical grading at the NFL Combine should incorporate shoulder instability, meniscal injury, ACL tear, and lower extremity injury history as these have well-documented effects on performance and availability.
athleteDev: NFL Combine medical evaluations should weigh history of shoulder injury, lumbar spine conditions, ACL reconstruction, and foot pathology as these are associated with reduced draft probability, diminished performance, and shorter careers. Physical performance at the Combine is not diminished by isolated ACL reconstruction history.
rtp: RTP benchmarks by injury type: ACL reconstruction 60–90% (position-dependent, ~6 months minimum); shoulder instability surgery 80–90% RTP but ~9 months; ACDF ~70% RTP; Achilles tendon rupture 66–72% RTP with 2–3 years of performance decrement; lateral meniscectomy 60% RTP at 8–9 months; lumbar discectomy 75–80% RTP. Corticosteroid injection for syndesmosis and muscle strains allows earlier RTP by approximately 10 days.

citation: Wang A, Healy J, Hyett N, Berthelot G, Kryger KO. A systematic review on methodological variation in acute:chronic workload research in elite male football players. Sci Med Football. 2020. DOI: 10.1080/24733938.2020.1765007.
abstract: This systematic review investigated methodological variation in acute:chronic workload ratio (ACWR) research and its relationship to injury in elite male soccer players. After searching four databases, 12 studies met inclusion criteria from 2,689 initial results. All studies were deemed poor quality by Newcastle-Ottawa Scale assessment. Substantial heterogeneity was found in workload definitions, GPS speed zone thresholds, ACWR time windows, and statistical methods, precluding meaningful cross-study comparison.
tldr: Note: This paper pertains to soccer/association football, not American football. Major methodological inconsistencies across 12 ACWR-injury studies in elite male soccer players make it impossible to draw firm conclusions about whether acute:chronic workload ratios reliably predict injury risk, underscoring the need for standardized protocols before ACWR is used operationally.
methods: PRISMA-guided systematic review of four databases (PubMed, Web of Science, ScienceDirect, Embase); included longitudinal cohort studies of elite male soccer players examining ACWR and injury rate/risk; quality assessed via Newcastle-Ottawa Scale; data extracted on population characteristics, workload protocol, injury classification, and statistical analysis.
findings:
• 12 studies included; all rated poor quality by Newcastle-Ottawa Scale
• Five studies used GPS-derived external workload; heterogeneity in speed zone definitions across all studies
• Nine studies incorporated sRPE as internal workload measure
• All studies used rolling average ACWR with 1:2, 1:3, or 1:4-week windows
• No consensus on exposure types (training only vs. training + matches + recovery sessions)
• Significant variation in injury classification, missing data handling, and statistical approaches
• None of the included studies received NOS points for comparability
limitations: All 12 included studies were of poor methodological quality; no standardized ACWR definition or workload measure exists; FIFA match GPS restrictions prevented data collection in two studies; heterogeneity precluded meta-analysis.
practicalImplications: Sport scientists using ACWR to monitor injury risk in football players should be cautious given the lack of methodological consensus. Practitioners should clearly define acute and chronic windows, standardize speed zone thresholds for GPS-derived external load, and specify which exposure types are included. ACWR may not be a reliable standalone injury prediction tool.
athleteDev: The methodological concerns raised — inconsistent workload definitions, variable ACWR windows, lack of standardization — are broadly applicable to any team sport load monitoring program including American football. Practitioners implementing ACWR-based athlete monitoring should develop consistent internal protocols rather than relying on published benchmarks from heterogeneous soccer research.
rtp: The lack of standardized injury definitions and RTP decision-making criteria across the 12 studies highlights that even injury classification varies substantially, complicating development of evidence-based RTP protocols in football contexts.

citation: Philipp NM, Crawford DA, Fry AC. A total score of athleticism to estimate the amount of variance explained in on-field performance within collegiate American football players. Int J Strength Cond. 2022. DOI: 10.47206/ijsc.v2i1.04.
abstract: This study aimed to create position-specific statistical models using physical performance tests to explain variance in on-field performance across four collegiate American football seasons at an NCAA Division 2 school. Four position groups were analyzed: Wide Receiver/Tight End, Running Back, Linebacker/Defensive Line, and Defensive Backs. Off-season tests included back squat, bench press, hang clean, 40-yard dash, pro-agility, L-drill, vertical jump, and broad jump. Linear mixed models with backward selection explained 22–49% of variance in on-field performance.
tldr: Position-specific models combining at least three physical performance tests (e.g., broad jump, hang clean, 40-yard dash, squat, L-drill) explain 22–49% of on-field performance variance in collegiate American football players, confirming that no single test captures overall athletic ability and that physical preparation priorities should differ by position.
methods: Retrospective analysis of preexisting off-season test data and individual player game statistics from four NCAA Division 2 football seasons (2016–2019); data standardized to z-scores by position group; linear mixed models with backward selection using AIC and marginal/conditional r²; Total Score of Performance calculated as mean of position-specific standardized game statistics.
findings:
• WR/TE model: broad jump, bench press, L-drill, hang clean, pro-agility explained 48.7% of variance (ICC=0.711)
• Running Back model: squat, 40-yard dash, broad jump explained 48% of variance (ICC=0.789)
• Defensive Back model: squat, 40-yard dash, vertical jump, L-drill explained 22% of variance
• LB/DL model: squat, hang clean, L-drill, pro-agility explained 47.8% of variance
• LB/DL group showed large gap between marginal (48%) and conditional (81%) r², suggesting interindividual random factors are important
limitations: Offensive linemen excluded due to lack of quantifiable on-field statistics; quarterbacks excluded due to small sample size; single NCAA Division 2 institution; does not account for coaching quality, scheme, or opponent strength.
practicalImplications: S&C staff should use position-specific test batteries rather than a single metric when profiling athletes and making training decisions. Hang clean and L-drill are particularly relevant for LB/DL; broad jump and 40-yard dash for skill positions. The large unexplained variance in most models suggests physical preparation alone is insufficient — coaching, tactical awareness, and motivation also drive performance.
athleteDev: Physical performance tests used at the NFL Combine (40-yard dash, broad jump, L-drill, pro-agility) are meaningful performance predictors when combined into position-specific models. Teams can use these findings to prioritize testing battery components, individualize off-season training programming, and support talent identification decisions.
rtp: The position-specific physical profiles identified could inform rehabilitation benchmarks and readiness-to-return criteria by establishing normative physical performance standards for each position group.

citation: Baker LB, Alfred S, Lee KA, et al. Accuracy of a smart bottle in measuring fluid intake by American football players during pre-season training. Sci Rep. 2023;13:11383.
abstract: This study determined the accuracy of a smart squeeze bottle (Gatorade Smart G² Bottle) equipped with optical sensor cap technology in measuring fluid intake in 30 male American football players during three outdoor pre-season practice sessions. Smart bottle measurements were compared to gravimetric reference measurements across 110 measurement time points. There was no significant mean difference in total fluid intake between methods, with Bland-Altman 95% limits of agreement of approximately ±15% of overall fluid intake, confirming the device as a valid real-world hydration monitoring tool.
tldr: A smart squeeze bottle with optical sensor cap technology accurately measured fluid intake in American football players during pre-season practice at the group level (no significant bias) with individual-level error of approximately ±15%, making it a practical real-time hydration monitoring tool for sport practitioners.
methods: 30 male American football players at IMG Academy participated in three outdoor pre-season practice sessions (~29°C, ~75% RH) during one week in August. Reference fluid intake measured by gravimetry; smart bottle used optical Time-of-Flight sensors and Bluetooth. Bland-Altman analysis, paired t-tests, Deming regression used; body mass measured pre/post practice.
findings:
• No significant mean difference in total fluid intake between smart bottle (1220±371 ml) and reference method (1236±389 ml; p=0.39)
• Mean difference expressed as percentage: −0.6±7.5%
• Bland-Altman 95% LOA: −212 to 180 ml (~±15% of total fluid intake)
• Strong correlation between methods (r=0.96, p<0.0001)
• Mean fluid balance: −0.64±0.73% body mass loss
• Sweating rates ranged 0.47–2.51 L/h; total sweat losses 0.77–3.72 L
limitations: Each player tested only once per day; smart bottle requires resting on a flat surface within 10 m of a paired smartphone; large temperature variations may affect sensor accuracy; study funded by Gatorade Sports Science Institute (PepsiCo R&D), a potential conflict of interest.
practicalImplications: Sport scientists and athletic trainers can use smart bottle technology to monitor real-time fluid intake in American football players during practice with acceptable accuracy. Combined with individualized sweat testing, smart bottles could help practitioners close the loop on real-time hydration during high-risk summer pre-season environments.
athleteDev: Pre-season American football practice involves substantial sweat losses (0.47–2.51 L/h), and wide inter-individual variation in fluid balance was observed. Smart bottle monitoring enables individualized hydration tracking at scale across a team roster during pre-season camp, supporting performance and safety.
rtp: Monitoring fluid balance and hydration status is relevant to RTP contexts — dehydration can impair neurocognitive function and physical performance, and ensuring adequate rehydration between sessions is a component of recovery and safe participation readiness for athletes returning from any time-loss event.

citation: Terry DP, Grashow R, Iverson GL, et al. Age of first exposure does not relate to post-career health in former professional American-style football players. Sports Med. 2024. DOI: 10.1007/s40279-024-02062-9.
abstract: This study examined whether age of first exposure (AFE) to American football before age 12 was associated with adverse later-life health outcomes in a large cohort of 4,189 former professional American-style football players from the Football Players Health Study (FPHS). Multivariable regressions adjusting for age, race, BMI, playing position, professional seasons, and prior concussion burden revealed no significant associations between AFE<12 and any of 17 outcomes including depression, anxiety, cognitive difficulties, dementia, hypertension, sleep apnea, chronic pain, memory loss, and ADHD.
tldr: In the largest study of its kind (n=4,189 former NFL players), starting tackle football before age 12 was not independently associated with worse post-career health outcomes after adjusting for relevant covariates, contradicting earlier small-sample studies.
methods: Cross-sectional survey study within the FPHS at Harvard University; 4,189 former professional American-style football players; AFE dichotomized at age 12 and also examined as a continuous variable; outcomes assessed via PHQ-2, GAD-2, Neuro-QoL scales, and self-reported medical diagnoses; multivariable linear and logistic regressions with penalized spline regression in generalized additive models to test non-linear AFE relationships.
findings:
• n=4,189 former players; mean age 52±14 years; 39% self-identified as Black
• Univariable: AFE<12 associated with slightly greater depression (p=0.03, η²=0.001) and anxiety — negligible effect sizes
• Multivariable: AFE<12 not significantly associated with any of 17 outcomes
• AFE<12 group reported lower rates of high blood pressure, sleep apnea, and low testosterone prescriptions
• No non-linear AFE relationships detected in spline models
• Concussion burden (cumulative signs and symptoms score) was a key covariate and meaningful determinant of adverse outcomes
limitations: Self-reported AFE and health outcomes subject to recall bias; medical diagnoses not validated against medical records; voluntary participation introduces ascertainment and healthy-worker bias; cross-sectional design; limited to former professional players.
practicalImplications: Clinicians and sport medicine staff evaluating former NFL players should focus on concussion burden, BMI, and playing position (lineman status) as more meaningful covariates of adverse health outcomes than the age at which a player began tackle football.
athleteDev: This study challenges prior research suggesting that youth tackle football participation before age 12 leads to irreversible long-term neurological harm in those who go on to play professionally. Player development staff can be reassured that early football participation was not independently linked to worse cognitive or psychiatric outcomes in this large sample.
rtp: Concussion burden — not age of first exposure — appears to be a more meaningful determinant of long-term health, underscoring the value of evidence-based concussion RTP protocols throughout an athlete's football career.
